coredata.dc:title: Effect of CQDs doping on the properties of RuO2–TiO2/Ti anode
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhengyi
Han, Zhaohui
Xu, Yang
Wei, Sikang
Zhou, Shenggang
Xu, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Phenol is a harmful substance that can cause pathological changes in animals or human body, and its degradation has become a research hotspot at present. This paper innovatively proposed the preparation of carbon quantum dots by microwave enhancement technology, the preparation of RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti composite coating anode by thermal decomposition method, and studied the electrocatalytic degradation process of organic wastewater containing phenol. The surface morphology of the anode was observed by XPS, TEM and SEM. Electrochemical workstation was used to characterize the electrocatalytic performance of the anode, and high performance liquid chromatography was used to determine the degradation process. The results show that the doping of CQDs can produce a large number of vacancy defects on the surface of the composite coating, and the addition of CQDs can increase the loading of Ru element on the surface of the coating during anode preparation. With the increase of CQDs content, the crack width and depth of coating surface decrease gradually, and the crystal size decreases. Doping of CQDs can effectively improve the surface charge capacity and corrosion resistance of anode. Compared with the traditional RuO2–TiO2/Ti anode, the removal efficiency of phenol and COD at the RuO2–TiO2-CQDS/Ti composite coating electrode was increased by 102% and 55.7% respectively within 1 h. This study makes full use of the high specific surface area and excellent electrocatalytic performance of carbon quantum dots, which provides a new idea for the mineralization of high concentration phenol in Ru–Ti electrode.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anode
Electrocatalytic oxidation technology
Phenol degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Anode XPS of RuO2–TiO2/Ti and RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti.
EPR diagram of RuO2–TiO2-CQDS/Ti and RuO2–TiO2/Ti anodes.
TEM images of RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti and RuO2–TiO2/Ti anodes.
EDS diagram of RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti and RuO2–TiO2/Ti anodes.
SEM images of anode with different CQDs content.
XRD patterns of RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anode with different CQDs contents.
CV curves of RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anodes with different CQDs contents.
LSV curves of RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anodes with different CQDs contents.
LSV curves of RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anodes with different CQDs contents.
Removal effects of phenol and COD by anodes with different CQDs contents.
Coating grain sizes of RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anodes with different CQDs contents.
Surface charge capacity of RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anodes with different CQDs contents.
Polarization potential, self-corrosion potential and self-corrosion current density of RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anodes with different CQDs contents.

head.ce:title: Effect of CQDs doping on the properties of RuO2–TiO2/Ti anode

Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phenol is a harmful substance that can cause pathological changes in animals or human body, and its degradation has become a research hotspot at present. This paper innovatively proposed the preparation of carbon quantum dots by microwave enhancement technology, the preparation of RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti composite coating anode by thermal decomposition method, and studied the electrocatalytic degradation process of organic wastewater containing phenol. The surface morphology of the anode was observed by XPS, TEM and SEM. Electrochemical workstation was used to characterize the electrocatalytic performance of the anode, and high performance liquid chromatography was used to determine the degradation process. The results show that the doping of CQDs can produce a large number of vacancy defects on the surface of the composite coating, and the addition of CQDs can increase the loading of Ru element on the surface of the coating during anode preparation. With the increase of CQDs content, the crack width and depth of coating surface decrease gradually, and the crystal size decreases. Doping of CQDs can effectively improve the surface charge capacity and corrosion resistance of anode. Compared with the traditional RuO2–TiO2/Ti anode, the removal efficiency of phenol and COD at the RuO2–TiO2-CQDS/Ti composite coating electrode was increased by 102% and 55.7% respectively within 1 h. This study makes full use of the high specific surface area and excellent electrocatalytic performance of carbon quantum dots, which provides a new idea for the mineralization of high concentration phenol in Ru–Ti electrode.

ce:section.ce:para: Phenol (hydroxybenzene) is a colorless crystalline substance that is contained in the list of priority controlled pollutants. It has a distinctive odor and is soluble in water and organic solvents [1]. Phenol is mainly derived from the chemical production of alkyl phenol, cresol, xylophone, phenolic resin, aniline and other compounds. It is likewise produced in the production of pesticides, explosives, dyes and textiles, as well as in the processing of petroleum, coal and metallurgy. In addition, the release of automobile exhaust is another important way for phenol to enter the environment [2]. In the United States alone, 580,000 people are susceptible to phenol at work. Once phenol enters the food chain. It can cause mutagenicity and carcinogenicity in animals and humans. If animals or humans are exposed to phenol for a long time, it will respond to the amino acids in the skin, resulting in the destruction of skin tissues and even pathological changes [3,4]. In addition, phenol can also damage animal and human organs, eyes, nerve centers, etc [5]. In adult males, 1 g phenol may be fatal [6]. High concentrations of phenol can cause throat dryness, lung edema, and even death. People exposed to phenol for a long time will show varying degrees of dizziness, loss of appetite, diarrhea and other symptoms [7,8].

Due to its advantages of high efficiency, economy and good compatibility, electrocatalytic oxidation technology has been widely applied in the treatment field of wastewater containing phenol and is considered to be an effective tool to decompose organic compounds that are most difficult to degrade [9]. Titanium anode, as the most common material used in electrocatalytic oxidation technology, plays a huge role in economic development and plays an extremely significant role in the field of phenol wastewater [10–13]. Such as: Wan et al. [14] studied the phenol removal rates of Ti/SnO2–Sb/PbO2, Ti/SnO2–Sb/Bi3+-PbO2 and Ti/SnO2–Sb/Bi5+-PbO2, and the results showed that, Ti/SnO2–Sb/Bi5+-PbO2 anode has the highest electrocatalytic activity and oxygen extraction potential, and its phenol removal rate can reach 99.65% and COD removal rate can reach 95.92% within 30 h. Yu [15] et al. prepared a Ti/RuO2–IrO2-RhOx electrode to oxidize phenol. The results showed that the service life of Ti/RuO2–IrO2-RhOx was 3.5 times that of conventional Ti/RuO2–IrO2, and the degradation efficiency of phenol was 1.2–2.7 times that of Ti/RuO2–IrO2. Li et al. [16] studied the electrochemical degradation of phenol by Ti/RuO2–Pt and Ti/IrO2–Pt two typical anodes, and found that Ti/RuO2–Pt anode had higher oxygen evolution potential than Ti/IrO2–Pt anode. The degradation rate of phenol on Ti/IrO2–Pt anode was slower than that on Ti/RuO2–Pt anode.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have a high specific surface area and more surface active centers, and CQDs can be used as both electron donors and acceptors [17]. In addition, CQDs has a large number of sp2 hybridized orbitals, which can accelerate electron migration, and these characteristics turn it into a hot spot in the research of electrochemical materials [18]. So far, CQDs has been applied to electrochemical detection of heavy metal ions, dopamine, l-cysteine, curcumin and antipsychotics [19–23]. These results indicate that CQDs and their hybrid materials or minicomputer have great application prospects in the field of electrochemistry. Therefore, in this study, microwave enhancement technology was utilized to prepare carbon quantum dots, and combined with thermal decomposition method, RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti composite coating electrode was prepared. The effects of CQDs doping on electrochemical performance, corrosion resistance and degradation rate of phenol were investigated. A new Ru–Ti anode with low cost was developed to improve the degradation rate of high concentration phenol.

The substances involved in this experiment include RuCl3·3H2O with 37% Ru mass fraction, butyl titanate (analytical pure), H2C2O4(analytical pure), NaOH (analytical pure), HCl(36%), N-butanol (analytical pure), glucose (analytical pure) and Na2SO4 (analytical pure). The titanium plate adopts TA2 titanium plate with the size of 10 mm × 1mm × 10 mm.

In this experiment, CQDs were prepared by microwave enhancement technology. Using glucose as carbon source, glucose solution with mass fraction of 3% was established and put into microwave heating chamber. The microwave heating rate is 12 °C·min−1, the heating time is 15min, and then the heat preservation time is 20min at 180 °C, and then it is taken out after natural cooling in the microwave box. The removed CQDs is necessary to freeze-dried and prepared into powder.

The titanium plate was polished to increase the roughness and remove surface stains and oxide layer, then washed with 10% NaOH alkali solution at 50 °C for 30min, and finally etched with 10% oxalic acid solution at 80 °C for 60min. The freeze-dried CQDs powder was decomposed into n-butanol with the mass fraction of 0%, 3.4%, 6.7% and 9.7%, and then appropriate HCl was added, and RuCl3 and butyl titanate were mixed into the coating solution with the substance ratio of n(Ru): n(Ti) = 3:7. The prepared coating liquid was evenly coated on the surface of the treated titanium plate with a fine brush, then dried in a drying oven at 120 °C for 10min, and then transferred to a Muffle oven at 500 °C for 10min. After natural cooling, the coating liquid was implemented, dried and roasted again for at least 15 times until the coating liquid was used up. The last coating was sintered at 500 °C for 1 h.

Shanghai Chenhua CHI-660E electrochemical workstation was used for the electrochemical test. A three-electrode system was adopted, with working electrode RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti electrode, the test area was 1 cm2, the auxiliary electrode was 1 cm2 platinum electrode, the reference electrode was mercury oxide, the electrolyte was saturated KCl solution, and the test temperature was normal temperature. The scanning rate of Tafel curve test is 10 mV/s, and the voltage range is 0.1–1.3 V. The scanning rate of the CV curve test is 50 mV/s, and the voltage range is 0.1–1.3 V. The scanning rate of LSV is 50 mV/s, and the voltage range is 0.1–1.5 V. Sensitivity was 0.001 for all.

The composition of titanium anode was analysed by XRD (X Pert PROMPD) diffractometer and XPS (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha). The XRD test results were analysed and processed by Jade6.5 software. The surface morphology of titanium anode was respected by SEM (VEGA-3SBH) and TEM (Tecnai G2 TF30), and the prepared CQDs were characterized by TEM and XPS.

High performance liquid chromatography (Waters 2996, USA) was performed on Waters RP18 (5 μm, 3.9 mm × 150 mm) column with diode array detector at a flow rate of 1.00 mL min−1. The mobile phase is 50% water and 50% methanol (v/v). The column temperature was 35 °C and λ = 254 nm. Chemical oxygen demand (COD) was measured by potassium dichromate titration [24].

In order to study the micromorphology of carbon quantum dots prepared by microwave strengthening technology, the micromorphology and structure of freeze-dried carbon quantum dots are studied by TEM and XPS in this study, and the particle diameter is measured by Nano-measuere software. The results are shown in Figs. 1 and 2 .

As can be observed in Fig. 1, the shape of CQDs is uniform and quasi-spherical, and there is no obvious agglomeration between CQDs, showing good dispersion. The particle diameter of CQDs ranges from 2.25 to 6.75 nm, with an average particle diameter of 4.25 nm. Fig. 2
is the XPS diagram of CQDs, as showing in Fig. 2. CQDs mainly consists of C and O elements. Depending on the high resolution spectrum of C1s, C1s has Csp2 (284.51eV), C–O (285.26eV), CO (286.7eV), and COOH (288.7eV). It can be seen from O1s high-resolution spectrum that there are functional groups of CO (530.84eV), C–O (532.28eV) and COOH (533.43eV) in CQDs. This suggests that the surface of carbon quantum dots is rich in hydroxyl, carboxyl and some aromatic compounds (see Fig. 3).

In order to analyze the chemical valence state of oxide coating in the prepared composite coating electrode, XPS analysis was performed on the prepared electrode, and the results were shown in Fig. 4 . It can be seen from Fig. 4 that the XPS analysis diagram of both RuO2–TiO2/Ti anodes and RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anodes consists of O1s, C1s, Ti2p, Ru3d, and Ru3p. From the high-resolution spectrum of O1s, it can be seen that the first peak at 529.7eV belongs to lattice oxygen, while the weak peak at 532.4eV comes from organic oxygen (for RuO2–TiO2/Ti anodes, the peak is 532.3eV), and for RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anodes, The high-resolution spectrum of O1s also has a peak at 531.2eV caused by vacancy defects [25], which is related to a large number of vacancy defects and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CQDs. The valence states corresponding to the peaks of 458.34eV and 464.2eV in the Ti2p high-resolution spectra are Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2, respectively, and the binding energy difference between them is 5.86eV, which is consistent with the expected oxidation state of Ti4+ in TiO2 [11]. It is worth noting that there is a peak of strong Ti3+ oxidation at 461.5 eV, which indicates the existence of oxygen vacancies and the possible lattice defects caused by oxygen vacancies [10]. For RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anodes, Ti2p orbits also have peaks at 460.6eV and 460eV caused by the presence of Ru4+. According to the high-resolution spectrum of Ru3p, Ru3p has two peaks, Ru3p3/2 (458.34eV) and Ru3p1/2 (464.2eV), among which Ru3p3/2 corresponds to Ru3+ and Ru3p1/2 corresponds to Ru4+ [16]. For RuO2–TiO2/Ti anodes, The peak at 461.28eV can be attributed to Ti4+, but for the RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anode, the doping of CQDs makes the peak redshift (about 0.99eV), which may be due to the coordination between CQDs and Ru4+ and Ru3+ ions, so that CQDs is fixed on the surface of the metal oxide. There are also a large number of sp2 hybridized orbitals in CQDs, which makes Ru3p redshift. It can be seen from the Ru3d high-resolution spectrum that the binding energy of Ru3d5/2 is low, 280.62 eV, corresponding to the oxidation state of Ru4+ [5]. The chemical state at 284.8eV is Ru3d3/2, which is just the same as the C1s binding energy of element C, which also indicates that CQDs has been successfully doped into the coating (various functional groups on the surface of CQDs will provide C elements, and the empty d orbital existing in Ru can bind with the surface functional groups of CQDs. Stable CQDs−metal ion coordination composites are generated [26]), and the peaks of RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anodes at 281.34eV and 282.24eV are also attributed to the surface functional groups of CQDs.

In order to determine the effect of CQDs doping on oxygen availability in the anode, electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy (EPR) was performed on RuO2–TiO2-CQDS/Ti and RuO2–TiO2/Ti anodes, and the results were shown in Fig. 5 .

As can be seen from Fig. 5, the g factor of oxygen vacancy in RuO2–TiO2-CQDS/Ti anode is 2.002, which is consistent with the literature [25], while RuO2–TiO2/Ti has no obvious symmetry, indicating that there is no obvious oxygen vacancy in RuO2–TiO2/Ti anode. Moreover, it can be seen from the EPR spectrum of RuO2–TiO2-CQDS/Ti anode that in the latter part of the spectrum (g value after 10), the spectrum tends to be smooth and stable (tends to 0), which indicates that CQDs is only doped into the electrode coating, and no substitution reaction occurs.

Transmission electron microscopy is often used to observe the submicrostructure or ultrastructure of materials, and with DES analysis, the content of elements on the surface of materials can be obtained. Figs. 6 and 7 show TEM and EDS of RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti and RuO2–TiO2/Ti anodes, respectively.

It can be seen from the observation in Fig. 6 and the XPS analysis in Fig. 4 that CQDs is successfully doped into the anode coating. EDS of anode coating doped with CQDs and without CQDs in Fig. 7 . According to Fig. 7, C element content in RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anode is much higher than that in RuO2–TiO2/Ti anode, which is consistent with the analysis results in Figs. 4 and 6. The content of Ru element is higher in RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anode, and the content of Ti element is lower. The results showed that the addition of CQDs increased the loading of Ru element, and thus increased the operational point of hydrogen evolution reaction on the surface of the coating. The reduction of Ti element indicates that the coverage rate of RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anode coating is higher than that of RuO2–TiO2/Ti anode, which reduces the risk of contact between titanium substrate and electrolyte and improves the stability of anode.

Fig. 8 shows in the surface topography of RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anode doped with different contents of CQDs. It can be observed in the figure that the microscopic morphology of the anode coating doped with different contents of CQDs is typical mud-like crack morphology, with a large number of cracks on the surface, and the cracks are relatively smooth. The size of the crack block and the width and depth of the cracks of different samples is also different [27]. With the increase of CQDs content, the crack width and depth decreased gradually. The formation of cracks is due primarily to the difference in thermal expansion coefficient between the rare metal oxide coating and the base metal in the heating process, resulting in thermal stress between the base metal and the oxide coating, leading to cracks [28]. The appearance of cracks indirectly increases the specific surface area of the anode. Thus reducing the current density and improving the electrode activity [29]. But at the same time, the increase of crack width also leads to the decrease of bonding force between the coating and the matrix, and the increase of Ti matrix exposure, thus reducing the service life of the coating. In addition, reaction bubbles will also enter the crack, best, generate stress, and cause the loss of the layer [30]. However, due to the abundant functional groups on the surface of CQDs (e.g. –OH,-CHO, etc.), combined with metal oxides (RuO2,TiO2) through ion exchange or coordination reaction, so that CQDs can adsorb metal oxides on the surface, modify the oxide of the anode coating, form CQDs-metal oxide nanometres, and reduce the difference of thermal expansion coefficient between metal oxides. Thus, the width and depth of cracks are reduced [31]. The residual oxygen-containing groups in cqds-metal oxides enable them to have good dispersion and long-term stability in water [32]. In addition, the small size of CQDs can provide a larger specific surface area for electrocatalytic reactions, and the rapid transfer of electrons on the electrode surface can further improve the electrocatalytic activity [33].

Fig. 9 shows XRD patterns of RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anodes with different CQDs contents. RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anode surface coating phase is primarily rutile phase. It can be observed in Fig. 9 that no Ti peak of the substrate appears in the XRD pattern of the measured anode, indicating that the anode surface coating prepared in this experiment has a high coverage rate, which reduces the contact between the electrolyte and the substrate and improves the service life and corrosion resistance of the anode, which is consistent with the analysis results in Fig. 6. To 2θ of 28°, 35°, 54.3°, the corresponding peaks are rutile [110], RuO2 [101], and TiO2 [211] crystal faces, respectively. According to Scherrer's formula [34], the grain sizes of different anode coatings were calculated as showed in Table 1 . When CQDs is doped into the coating, the crystal size begins to decrease with the increase of CQDs content, which is due to the defect structure and CQDs caused by oxygen vacancy that hinders the growth of crystal size [35].

Cyclic voltammetry (CV curve), as an extremely important characteristic of electrochemical performance, is often used to determine the number of active points on the anode surface [36]. The principle is that the area enclosed by the anode CV curve is proportional to the surface charge capacity Q of the coating, and the value of Q reflects the number of active points on the surface of the anode [37], while the area enclosed by the CV curve is related to the surface structure of the coating. The greater the relative area of the coating surface (width and depth of surface cracks), the greater the area enclosed by the CV curve. As shown in Fig. 10 , it is the anode CV curve of doped CQDs with different content.

As can be seen from Fig. 10, obtained CV curves have similar shapes except for the closed region, indicating that the chemical reactions occurring on the anode surface are the same [38]. It is calculated according to formula Q = SA/v (where Q represents the electrode surface charge capacity, S represents the area of CV curve, A represents the area of the measured electrode (generally 1 cm2), and v represents the scanning rate), the anode surface charge capacity of doped CQDs with different contents is given in Table 2 . As can show in Table 2, when CQDs is doped into the anode coating, the surface charge capacity of the anode can be effectively improved. When the doping amount of CQDs is 3.4%, the surface charges capacity increases by 19.5%. This may be due to the fact that CQDs are good electron acceptors, and numerous functional groups exist on the surface of CQDs. When CQDs is combined with metal oxides in the coating, CQDs is fixed on the surface of the coating, thus improving the surface charge capacity of the anode [39]. When multiple transition metals are co-doped, modulation and synergistic effects between metal will amplify the effect, thus further enhancing the electrocatalytic performance [40]. With the increase of CQDs content, the area enclosed by the CV curve decreases, indicating that its surface charge capacity also decreases. The reasons include several aspects, one of which is that with the increase of CQDs content, the crack width of the surface coating decreases, resulting in the decrease of the specific surface area, and thus the surface charge capacity of the anode [41].

Polarization curve measurement (LSV) can indirectly reflect the catalytic activity of the electrode. The potential corresponding to the inflection point in the figure is the chlorine evolution potential (polarization potential) of the anode. The lower the chlorine evolution potential, the higher the catalytic activity of the electrode (when the polarization potential of the electrode is reduced by 100 mV, the electrocatalytic activity increases by 10 time [42]). Therefore, the polarization potential of the chlorine evolution anode should be decreased as much as possible to improve the catalytic activity. Fig. 11 displays the LSV curve of the anode doped with different CQDs content, and Table 3 shows the polarization potential of the electrode with different CQDs content.

It can be observed in Fig. 11 and Table 3 that the polarization potential gradually decreases with the increase of CQDs content, which indicates that the doping of CQDs helps to improve the electrocatalytic activity of the electrode. When the content of CQDs reaches 9.7%, the polarization potential of the electrode decreases by 85 mV. This may be related to the fact that CQDs can be used as a good carrier to prevent the aggregation of metal or metal oxides, thus significantly improving the catalytic activity. The strong interaction inside the CQDs matrix composites is also beneficial to continue to improve the electrocatalytic performance by promoting intermolecular electron transfer.

Tafel curve is an essential test method for examining electrode materials. The corrosion condition of the electrode is affirmed in extrapolation of the curve. The corrosion resistance and the corrosion rate of the electrode can be explained by investigating the self-corrosion current density and corrosion potential of different electrode Tafel curves. The lower the self-corrosion current density and the higher the corrosion potential, the lower the corrosion rate and the stronger the corrosion resistance [43]. Fig. 12 shows the Tafel curve of RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti anodes doped with different CQDs contents.

According to Fig. 12 and Tables 3 and it can be seen that with the increase of CQDs content, the self-corrosion potential of the anode becomes higher and higher, and the self-corrosion current density becomes lower and lower, which indicates that the doping of CQDs helps to improve the corrosion resistance of the electrode. When the content of CQDs was increased to 9.7%, the self-corrosion potential of the anode was increased by 217% compared with that of the anode without CQDs doping. This may be due to the existence of insoluble CQDs layer on the coating layer, which can effectively protect the electrode in the electrolyte. Thus greatly improving the structural stability of RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti in the electrocatalytic process [44].

In order to study the degradation rate of catalytic degradation of organic compounds by doping different amounts of CQDs, this study took 1 g/L phenol concentration as the initial concentration, Na2SO4 as the electrolyte, and the phenol was degraded at a constant current power supply of 0.03 V/cm2. Samples were taken every 10min for a total of 6 times, with a total degradation time of 60min. In order to better describe the removal of sharp concentration phenol and its intermediates, COD was selected as the evaluation index to investigate the overall mineralization effect of phenol removal by electrocatalysis. The result is shown in Fig. 13
(see Fig. 14).

As can be seen from Fig. 13, with the increase of CQDs content, the removal efficiency of phenol and COD also increase. When the CQDs content reaches 9.7%, the degradation rate of phenol and COD increases by 102% and 55.7% was higher than that without doping. This is due to the fact that CQDs attach to the oxide after pairing with the metal oxide, and CQDs are good electron acceptors and donors, which can increase the electron transfer rate for the reaction, resulting in the production of more ∙OH, which promotes the formation of oxidant MOx(∙OH), MOx(∙OH) oxidizes phenol to produce CO2+H2O [45], which is an indirect oxidation reaction (IAO). Due to its presence in an electrified environment, electrolytic water reaction may also occur on the anode surface to generate a small amount of oxygen. As can be seen from Fig. 13, the degradation rate of COD is much lower than that of phenol (when CQDs doping is 9.7%, the degradation rate of phenol is 83.33%, while that of COD is only 53%), which may be due to the accumulation of degradation intermediates and undegraded phenol. On the cathode, H+ generated by O2 and anode reacts to produce H2O2, and Na2SO4 as electrolyte also has a positive effect on the degradation of phenol and COD, which can be attributed to the generation of particulate [46]. In the presence of electron donors (CQDs) at the anode, the H2O2 produced will be oxidized to ∙OH, which further improves the degradation rate of phenol.

In order to study the effect of carbon quantum dot doping on the performance of RuO2–TiO2/Ti coated electrode, this paper prepared carbon quantum dot by microwave enhancement technology, combined with thermal decomposition method to prepare RuO2–TiO2-CQDs/Ti composite coated electrode, and studied the degradation of phenol containing solution. The experimental results are shown below:
(1)
The surface morphology of the composite coating electrode was analysed by SEM and TEM. The results demonstrate that the crack width and depth decrease gradually with the increase of CQDs content. XPS was utilized to analyze the valence states of oxides on the surface of composite coatings. It was found that CQDs doping caused a large number of vacancy defects caused by oxygen-containing functional groups in the electrode surface. The crystal sizes decreases with the increase of CQDs content.
(2)
The electrochemical properties of composite coating electrodes prepared by cyclic voltammetry, polarization measurement and Tafel curve were studied. The results show that CQDs doping can effectively improve the surface charge capacity of the anode, and when the doping amount of CQDs is 3.4%, the surface charge capacity increases by 19.5%. The polarization potential is decreased with the increase of CQDs content. When the doping amount of CQDs reached 9.7%, the polarization potential of the electrode reduced by 85 mV. Doping of CQDs can improve the corrosion resistance of the electrode.
(3)
The phenol degradation test showed that the removal efficiency of phenol and COD increased with the increase of CQDs content. When the doping amount of CQDs reached 9.7%, the degradation rate of phenol and COD increased by 102% and 55.7% compared with RuO2–TiO2/Ti electrode.

Titanium anode doped with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was used to degrade high concentration phenol efficiently, and the good electrocatalytic performance of CQDs was fully utilized. However, the excellent performance of carbon quantum dots is far from this, and the modification of carbon quantum dots by impurity atoms may be able to provide better performance, which is also a follow-up research direction. Finally, it is hoped that this study can provide a new idea and guidance for the degradation of high concentration phenol.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study.
Zhengyi Li: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing-original draft.
Zhaohui Han: Visualization, Writing-review&editing, Funding acquisition. Shenggang.
Zhou, Yang Xu, SiKang Wei, Lei Xu: Visualization, Funding acquisition, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51864030, 52161024), and by Yunnan Provincial youth top-notch talent support program.
